request.question: Who's album which reflects on his depressive state, had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976?

title: Come to Me (France Joli song)
Love Machine (The Miracles song)
Bardeux
Lean into It
Extra Texture (Read All About It)
Jeff Dawson
Right Back Where We Started From (album)
Manu Gavassi (album)
Attitudes (band)
Welcome Back (John Sebastian album)
sentences: "Come to Me" is a #1 disco hit from 1979 performed by France Joli, who had recorded it at the age of fifteen with producer, Tony Green, who composed the song and briefly sings on it.
The track also features the famed Philadelphia session vocalists, The Sweethearts of Sigma Sound.
The song was introduced on the album "France Joli", which was released in the US on 17 April 1979 on Prelude, and rose to #26.
On the Billboard Hot 100.
"Come to Me" received a major boost on 7 July when Joli performed it as a last minute replacement for Donna Summer at a concert held on Fire Island, whose estimated audience numbered 5000.
"Come to Me" began a three-week reign atop the Billboard Hot Dance Club Play on 22 September 1979.

"Come to Me" peaked at #15 on the Hot 100 and at #36 on the R&B chart, a decent showing for a new artist, but also a symptom of the increasing disenchantment of Top 40 radio stations with disco music.
"Love Machine" is a 1975 single recorded by Motown group The Miracles, taken from their album "City of Angels".
This song was a #1 Pop smash on the "Billboard Hot 100", and the biggest-selling hit single of The Miracles' career.
This single was one of two Billboard Hot 100 Top 20 hits recorded by The Miracles with Billy Griffin as lead vocalist; the other is 1973's "Do It Baby".
Griffin had replaced Miracles founder Smokey Robinson as lead singer in 1972.
The song features a growling vocal by Miracle Bobby Rogers, with group baritone Ronnie White repeating ""Yeah Baby"" throughout the song.
Bardeux is a dance duo from the 1980s.
The group was formed in 1986 and consisted of Stacy "Acacia" Smith and Tairrie B.
The group's first single "Three Time Lover", went to #10 on Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart.
Tairrie B left the group to pursue a solo career and was replaced by Lisa "Jaz" Teaney.
In 1988 the group released their album "Bold as Love" and their second single "Magic Carpet Ride".
The single went to #5 on Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart and #81 on the "Billboard" Hot 100.
The group performed the song on Club MTV.
"When We Kiss", the third single from the album, would turn out to be the group's biggest hit going all the way to #36 on the Billboard Hot 100.
The fourth single released from the album, "Bleeding Heart", was a top 40 hit on the Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart.
A fifth and last single release from that album, "Hold Me, Hold Me" did not chart.
Lean into It is the second studio album by the American rock supergroup Mr. Big, released in 1991.

The band's breakthrough release, "Lean into It" peaked at number 15 on the "Billboard" 200 charts, while the single "To Be with You" became the band's first and only song to hit number 1 on the Billboard Hot 100.
The follow-up single, "Just Take My Heart", was another Top 40 hit, peaking at number 16 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Extra Texture (Read All About It) is the sixth studio album by English musician George Harrison, released in September 1975.
It was Harrison's final album under his contract with Apple Records and EMI, and the last studio album issued by Apple.
The release came nine months after his troubled 1974 North American tour with Ravi Shankar and the poorly received "Dark Horse" album.
The melancholic mood of the recording reflects Harrison's depressed state at the criticism generated by these projects.
Jeff Dawson is a Canadian record producer, song writer, engineer, and mixer.
Vancouver-based Jeff Dawson developed and produced the breakthrough self-titled second album for Daniel Powter.
After working on Daniel's songs and helping develop the project's sound over 3 years, Warner Bros. Chairman/CEO Tom Whalley took one listen and knew he had something extraordinary.
Daniel's single "Bad Day" was #1 for 5 weeks on Billboard Hot 100 of 2006 and has become an incredible world wide hit with almost 2.2 million downloads and nearly 3 million albums sold.
Dawson produced State of Shock's hit single "Money Honey" (certified platinum in Canada) cementing Top 10 status at multiple Canadian radio formats, including Rock, CHR, and Hot AC.

Along with becoming the #1 most played/requested song at Satellite radio selling 40,000 copies prior to its US release, surpassing platinum status for digital sales with over 51,000 sold, "Money Honey" also held down the #1 post on the Canadian all formats Radio Chart for two months, spending close to a year on the Canadian Billboard Hot 100 singles Chart.

Right Back Where We Started From is the debut album by British R&B and soul music singer Maxine Nightingale The title track was first released as a single in the autumn of 1975 and reached #8 in the UK .
In the US it reached #5 in Adult Contemporary, #9 in Dance Music/Club Play, #46 in R&B singles and #2 in the Billboard Hot 100.
It was the success of this single to which prompted her to return to London to complete a Right Back Where We Started From album.
The album was released in the US early 1976 to enthusiastic reaction, reaching #65 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Another single from the album, “Gotta Be the One”, charted at #53 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Manu Gavassi is the debut album of Brazilian singer-songwriter Manu Gavassi.
The album contains thirteen tracks, the sound is defined with strong influence on teen pop and pop rock.
The first single from the album was "Garoto Errado".
The song was released in June 11, 2011 and peaked at number 22 in Brazil's Billboard Hot 100.
The second single from the album was "Planos Impossíveis" and was released in January 25, 2011.
The song received positive reviews from critics and peaked at number 26 in Brazil's Billboard Hot 100.
The album was produced by Rick Bonadio and released through Universal Music Brazil's subsidiary, Midas Music, on October 12, 2010.
Attitudes was a rock band from Los Angeles consisting of keyboardist David Foster, guitarist Danny Kortchmar, bass guitarist Paul Stallworth and drummer Jim Keltner.
Its four members came together while working as session musicians during the recording of George Harrison's album "Extra Texture (Read All About It)".
One single by the band, "Sweet Summer Music" reached #94 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 in 1976.
James Taylor later covered their single "Honey Don't Leave L.A." and that version reached #61 on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1978.
Welcome Back is an album by American singer/songwriter John Sebastian, released in 1976 (see 1976 in music).
It reached number 79 on The Billboard 200 chart.
Two singles were released from the album with the title song "Welcome Back" reaching number one on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and Adult Contemporary charts.
It also reached number 93 on the Country Singles chart.
The second single, "Hideaway" peaked at number 95 on the Billboard Hot 100.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Who's album which reflects on his depressive state, had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976?
hop1_docs: Klique | Klique was an American R&B trio, consisting of Howard Huntsberry, Isaac Suthers and his sister, Deborah Suthers.  They released four albums, starting with "It's Winning Time" in 1981, concluding with "Love Cycles" in 1985.  Their only song to make the US "Billboard" Hot 100 chart was a cover version of Jackie Wilson's 1960 hit, "Stop Doggin' Me Around," which reached #50 on the Hot 100 and #2 on the US "Billboard" R&B chart in 1983.  In total, they had nine songs on "Billboard"' s R&B chart.

Break It to Me Gently (Aretha Franklin song) | "Break It to Me Gently" is a song written by Carole Bayer Sager and Marvin Hamlisch which was an R&B hit for Aretha Franklin in 1977.  Released from the "Sweet Passion" album, it reached Number 1 on the Hot Soul Singles chart in June 1977.  The song's success was only faintly reflected on the "Billboard" Hot 100 with a Number 85 peak, before dropping out of the Hot 100 after two weeks.  "Break It to Me Gently" would be Franklin's final Atlantic single to appear on the Hot 100 - from which she would be absent until 1980.

Jibbs | Jovan Campbell (born November 13, 1990), better known by his stage name Jibbs, is an American rapper.  He had one top ten single on the "Billboard" Hot 100, which was his debut single "Chain Hang Low".  Jibbs' only other song that charted on the "Billboard" Hot 100 is "King Kong" (featuring Chamillionaire).  Jibbs is currently working on his next studio album, "Ten Years Later", to be released in 2017.

Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song) | "Show Me the Way" is a song written by Peter Frampton, which was originally released on his 1975 album "Frampton" and as a single, but gained its highest popularity as a song from his 1976 live album "Frampton Comes Alive! ".  The song reached number 6 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and number 10 on the UK Singles Chart, becoming his biggest hit until "I'm in You" in 1977.

Tyga discography | The discography of Tyga, an American rapper, consists of four studio albums, two compilation albums, fourteen mixtapes, eight singles (including four as a featured artist) and forty-eight music videos.  In 2008, Tyga released his first studio album, "No Introduction", on the record label Decaydance Records.  The album featured the single "Coconut Juice", which features singer Travie McCoy; the song peaked at number 94 on the US "Billboard" Hot 100, becoming Tyga's first song to appear on the chart.  In 2010, Tyga and American singer Chris Brown released the collaborative mixtape "Fan of a Fan", which included the single "Deuces": the song peaked at number 14 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and became Tyga's first song to chart on the US Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs chart, peaking at number thirty.  Tyga also appeared on the song "Loyalty", a single by fellow rapper Birdman, and collaborated with rapper Lil Wayne on the non-album single "I'm on It", both of which failed to chart on the "Billboard" Hot 100.

Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright) | "Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)" is a song by Rod Stewart, recorded at Muscle Shoals Sound Studio in Sheffield, Alabama for his 1976 album "A Night on the Town".  The song became his second US chart topper on the "Billboard" Hot 100.  spending 8 weeks at #1 from November 13, 1976 - January 1, 1977, peaked at No. 5 in the UK, No. 3 in Australia and charted well in other parts of the world as well.  It was the number 1 song on "Billboard"' s 1977 year-end chart.  It became the best-selling single of 1977 in the United States.

Lil Wayne singles discography | American rapper Lil Wayne has released 200 singles – including 44 as a lead artist – and twelve promotional singles. Lil Wayne attained his first singles chart entry in 1999 as a featured artist on Hot Boys member Juvenile's single "Back That Azz Up", which peaked at number 19 on the United States "Billboard" Hot 100 and became a top ten hit on the Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs and Hot Rap Songs charts.  Wayne later released his debut solo studio album "Tha Block Is Hot" in November 1999.  Its title track and lead single, which features B.G. and Juvenile, reached number 65 on the "Billboard" Hot 100. " Lights Out" followed in December 2000 and produced the singles "Get Off the Corner", "Everything" and "Shine". " Way of Life", the lead single from Wayne's third studio album "500 Degreez", peaked at number 71 on the Hot 100 and became a top 20 hit on the Hot Rap Songs chart.  In 2004, Wayne was featured on the single "Soldier" by American girl group Destiny's Child, which became his first top ten hit on the Hot 100 and enjoyed commercial success internationally.
hop1_titles: Break It to Me Gently (Aretha Franklin song)
Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song)
response.summary_1: Evidence: passage [4] — "as a song from his 1976 live album 'Frampton Comes Alive!' . The song reached number 6 on the 'Billboard' Hot 100" (Peter Frampton); passage [6] — song "recorded ... for his 1976 album 'A Night on the Town'. The song became his second US chart topper on the 'Billboard' Hot 100, spending 8 weeks at #1 from November 13, 1976 - January 1, 1977" (Rod Stewart). Missing bridge: which album (and which artist) is described as "reflects on his depressive state." Next retrieval should find the album/artist described as reflecting on his depressive state and then determine whether that artist/album corresponds to one of the 1976 Hot 100 hits above.
response.hop2_query: album reflects on his depressive state Rod Stewart Peter Frampton A Night on the Town Frampton Comes Alive!
hop2_docs: Frampton Comes Alive! II | Frampton Comes Alive!  II is a 1995 live album by English musician Peter Frampton.  It is a sequel to his 1976 multiplatinum album "Frampton Comes Alive".  The title is styled as Frampton Comes Alive II on packaging.

Frampton Comes Alive! | Frampton Comes Alive!  is a double live album by English rock musician Peter Frampton released in 1976.  It is one of the best-selling live albums in the United States, and considered by many to be one of the finest live rock recordings of all time.  Following four solo albums with little commercial success, "Frampton Comes Alive!"  was a breakthrough for the artist.

Frampton (album) | Frampton is the fourth studio album by Peter Frampton, released in 1975 before he went on tour and recorded "Frampton Comes Alive! " The most recognizable songs from the album are "Show Me the Way" and "Baby, I Love Your Way" which became big hits when released as singles from "Frampton Comes Alive!"  The album peaked at #32 on the "Billboard" 200.

Live in San Francisco, March 24, 1975 | Peter Frampton, Live In San Francisco, March 24, 1975 is a live recording by Peter Frampton and the precursor to the Record Breaking "Frampton Comes Alive."  It's a much more intimate show, with a smaller audience, broadcast on San Francisco's KSAN from Sausalito's Record Plant, literally only months before he recorded the Live album, "Frampton Comes Alive," that would break records as one of the best-selling live albums in the United States.

Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song) | "Show Me the Way" is a song written by Peter Frampton, which was originally released on his 1975 album "Frampton" and as a single, but gained its highest popularity as a song from his 1976 live album "Frampton Comes Alive! ".  The song reached number 6 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and number 10 on the UK Singles Chart, becoming his biggest hit until "I'm in You" in 1977.

Hiatt Comes Alive at Budokan? | Hiatt Comes Alive at Budokan?  was singer-songwriter John Hiatt's twelfth album and first live album, released in 1994.  The album was actually recorded at venues in North America; the title parodies both Peter Frampton's "Frampton Comes Alive! " and the "At Budokan" albums released by numerous artists.

Peter Frampton | Peter Kenneth Frampton (born 22 April 1950) is an English rock musician, singer, songwriter, producer, and guitarist.  He was previously associated with the bands Humble Pie and The Herd.  At the end of his 'group' career was Frampton's international breakthrough album his live release, "Frampton Comes Alive! " The album sold in the United States more than 8 million copies and spawned several single hits.  Since then he has released several major albums.  He has also worked with David Bowie and both Matt Cameron and Mike McCready from Pearl Jam, among others.
hop2_titles: Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song)
response.summary_2: Peter Frampton's 1976 live album Frampton Comes Alive! included "Show Me the Way," which reached #6 on the Billboard Hot 100; the retrieved passages do not identify any album as "reflecting on his depressive state" or link that description to Frampton (or to Rod Stewart).
